retrieved_skills.skill_text: Hydro pole design and the fact that the address signage is blue with white numerals (seen on the curb with "164") matches the standard used in many Canadian provinces, especially Nova Scotia.
The condition of the road and sidewalk as well as the electric pole style matches rural settings in Mexico, especially Yucatán, Campeche, or Tabasco.
The vegetation includes a solitary papaya tree, walls with barbed wire, and lush greenery in the distance, characteristic of tropical climates.
The sidewalk and curb construction style, with its painted edge and sloped access points, is typical for small towns in southern Mexico.
The road has a single dashed yellow centerline with solid white outside edges, a marking pattern common in the United States and Mexico.
Given the road style and the type of desert vegetation, with small bushes and grass tufts, this matches the Chihuahuan Desert region.
The landscape is relatively flat with scattered trees and open pasture, supporting the idea of a rural area in a tropical country.
The climate appears temperate, with green grass and blue skies, fitting regions like southern Alberta, Saskatchewan, or Manitoba.
The architecture features flat-roofed, single-story houses with pastel colors, common in rural Mexico and Central America.
The dryness of the climate with yellow shrubbery is common along the regions of the US and Mexico border
The sky is clear and the lighting is very intense, supporting a location in central or northern Mexico.
The soil appears light-colored and somewhat sandy, indicating tropical or subtropical lowland terrain.

hard_clues: Yellow curb paint — commonly used in Latin America for no-parking zones
Roll-up metal garage doors and barred windows — typical of Central American towns
Corrugated metal roofing + pastel stucco walls — architectural style prevalent in Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador
Motorcycle as primary personal transport — consistent with rural/semi-urban Latin America
Vegetation: dense broadleaf trees suggest tropical highland or lowland region
Cloud pattern (altocumulus) + bright sun — indicates mid-latitude tropical zone, likely Central America
Absence of European/North American infrastructure (e.g., standardized signage, wide sidewalks, multiple lanes)
ambiguous_clues: License plate on sedan not legible — cannot confirm country
No visible language on signs — could be Spanish-speaking region but not definitive
Utility pole design generic — doesn’t uniquely identify country
Road width and condition could match many developing regions globally
Purple building color unusual — may be local preference rather than regional indicator
possible_scripts: Spanish (most likely — architecture, vegetation, infrastructure strongly suggest Central America)
Possibly Mayan languages spoken locally if in Guatemala (e.g., K’iche’, Q’eqchi’)
Portuguese unlikely — architecture and vegetation less typical of Brazil’s interior
English very unlikely — no colonial British/French/American infrastructure cues
